14	    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise

19	    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a

21	  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't

30	---
31	
32	# DAG Walker
33	
34	You take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not ∞), and (3) a `\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn ∞-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy — a node is only honestly "ready to prove" when its `\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.
35	
36	You are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes — across chapters — filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.
37	
38	## Directive Format
39	
40	```markdown
41	# DAG Walker Directive
42	
43	## Slug
44	<slug>
45	
46	## Seed
47	<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.
48	 May be an ∞-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>
49	
50	## Strategy context
51	<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted — what this cone supports in
52	 the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>
53	
54	## Depth / scope
55	<how far up to walk: "the whole cone to the axioms" (default), or "one level —
56	 the seed's direct \uses only", or "stop at chapter X". Plus any out-of-scope
57	 declarations.>
58	
59	## References
60	- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>
61	```
62	
63	## How you walk
64	
65	Use **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate — do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:
66	
67	```
68	archon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed
69	archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)
70	archon dag-query gaps --json                         # all ∞ holes, project-wide
71	```
72	
73	`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies — add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\leanok` / `\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).
74	
75	## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)
76	
77	1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or — if the dependency is real but unblueprinted — **add the missing `\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.
78	
79	2. **Has an informal proof — finite effort, not ∞.** A node whose `effort_local` is ∞ is a statement with **no `\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:
80	   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.
81	   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.
82	   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** — leave it ∞, record it under "Could not complete", and surface any strategy implication.
83	
84	3. **`\uses{}` is complete — the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\uses{lem:L}`, add it — and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise — remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.
85	
86	   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes — project lemmas it calls, instances it needs — are what `\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry — trivial statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}`, accurate `\uses{}`, one-line proof — and wire its consumers to `\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.
87	
88	A node is **complete** when it is `\leanok` / `\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\mathlibok` anchors — they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.
89	
90	## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)
91	
92	- **Never add `\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.
93	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\mathlibok`, and add a `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line — no `\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it ∞ and report it.
94	
95	## Citation discipline (the hard rule)
96	
97	Every block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (foreground Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain) and wait for it **inside this turn**: if the harness auto-backgrounds the dispatch and hands you a task ID, poll its report (`Monitor` on an until-loop; foreground `sleep` is blocked) until it lands — never end your turn assuming a still-running retriever returned. Then open the new `references/<slug>.md` and write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE — never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
98	
99	## What you must / must not do
100	
101	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
102	- **Respect protected material** (the "Protected by the mathematician" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine — other declarations may `\uses{}` their labels — but their own `\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in "Notes for dispatcher" instead of editing.
103	- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`).
104	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative — the blueprint reads as a standalone document.
105	- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\uses{}` and (except for declared "Could not complete" nodes) zero ∞ effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.
106	
107	## Workflow
108	
109	1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.
110	2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are ∞ (need a proof), which `\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.
111	3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.
112	4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.
113	5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.
114	6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / ∞ holes until only genuine gaps remain.
115	7. Write your report.
116	
117	## Logging
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).

140	- Proof sketch for `<label>` — <brief shape; ∞ → finite>
141	- Mathlib anchor `<label>` → `\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\mathlibok`
142	
143	## \uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)
144	- `<node>` now `\uses{<dep>}` — its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).
145	- Fixed broken `\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` → `<good>` / created `<dep>`.
146	
147	## Could not complete (genuine gaps — strategy items)
148	- `<label>` — no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left ∞. <strategy implication, if any>
149

The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

260	/-- **The substrate operation respects isomorphisms in both arguments.**
261	
262	A pair of isomorphisms `e : M ≅ M'` and `e' : N ≅ N'` in `X.Modules` induces an
263	isomorphism `tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N'`, obtained by sheafifying the
264	tensor product (in the presheaf-of-modules monoidal category) of the underlying
265	presheaf isomorphisms `e.val`, `e'.val`. Its `hom` is
266	`tensorObj_functoriality e.hom e'.hom`. This is the reusable functor-of-isos
267	ingredient feeding `tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial`. -/
268	noncomputable def tensorObjIsoOfIso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M M' N N' : X.Modules}
269	    (e : M ≅ M') (e' : N ≅ N') : tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj M' N' :=
270	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
271	    (MonoidalCategory.tensorIso
272	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat))
273	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e)
274	      ((SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf).mapIso e'))
275	
276	/-- **The substrate tensor of the structure sheaf with itself is the structure
277	sheaf.**
278	
279	`tensorObj 𝒪_X 𝒪_X ≅ 𝒪_X`, where `𝒪_X = SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf` is the
280	designated unit object. Built from the presheaf-level left unitor
281	`λ_ (𝟙_)` (the unit of the `PresheafOfModules` monoidal category is exactly
282	`SheafOfModules.unit.val`) under sheafification, composed with the
283	sheafification-adjunction counit isomorphism on the (already-sheaf) unit. -/
284	noncomputable def tensorObj_unit_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} :
285	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
286	      ≅ SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf :=
287	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
288	      (λ_ (𝟙_ (_root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)))) ≪≫
289	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app
290	      (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf)
291	
292	/-- **Left unitor for `⊗_X`.** `𝒪_X ⊗_X M ≅ M`. (Blueprint
293	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, left half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_left_unitor`.)
294	Sheafification of the presheaf-level left unitor `λ_ M.val`, composed with the
295	sheafification counit identifying `sheafification M.val` with the (already-sheaf)
296	`M`. The cheap `mapIso` pattern; uses no abstract pullback. -/
297	noncomputable def tensorObj_left_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
298	    tensorObj (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) M ≅ M :=
299	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
300	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).leftUnitor M.val) ≪≫
301	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
302	
303	/-- **Right unitor for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X 𝒪_X ≅ M`. (Blueprint
304	`lem:tensorobj_unit_iso`, right half, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_right_unitor`.)
305	Sheafification of the presheaf-level right unitor `ρ_ M.val`, composed with the
306	sheafification counit. -/
307	noncomputable def tensorObj_right_unitor {X : Scheme.{u}} (M : X.Modules) :
308	    tensorObj M (SheafOfModules.unit X.ringCatSheaf) ≅ M :=
309	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
310	      ((PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct (R := X.presheaf)).rightUnitor M.val) ≪≫
311	    (asIso (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).counit).app M
312	
313	/-- **Braiding for `⊗_X`.** `M ⊗_X N ≅ N ⊗_X M`. (Blueprint
314	`lem:tensorobj_comm_iso`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_braiding`.)
315	The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category is symmetric; its braiding `β_ M.val
316	N.val` sheafifies to the asserted isomorphism by the cheap `mapIso` pattern. -/
317	noncomputable def tensorObj_braiding {X : Scheme.{u}} (M N : X.Modules) :
318	    tensorObj M N ≅ tensorObj N M :=
319	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (R := X.ringCatSheaf) (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)).mapIso
320	    (BraidedCategory.braiding
321	      (C := _root_.PresheafOfModules (X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat)) M.val N.val)
322	
323	/-- **Associator for `⊗_X`.** (Blueprint
324	`lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso`.)
325	For arbitrary `M, N, P : X.Modules` there is an isomorphism
326	`(M ⊗_X N) ⊗_X P ≅ M ⊗_X (N ⊗_X P)`. This is the objectwise existence-of-iso datum the
327	group law consumes (associativity as a `Nonempty (… ≅ …)`).
328	
329	**UNCONDITIONAL and axiom-clean (iter-238 ROUTE (d)).** No flatness or local-triviality
330	hypothesis is used: the earlier flatness route (`W_whisker{Right,Left}_of_flat`, needing
331	sectionwise flatness — false for a general line bundle on a non-affine open) is RETIRED.
332	Writing `a = PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.val)` and `η` the
333	sheafification-adjunction unit, the three-step composite is:
334	  1. `a(η_{M.val ⊗ᵖ N.val} ▷ P.val)` is iso, giving
335	     `(M ⊗ N) ⊗ P ≅ a((M.val⊗N.val) ⊗ P.val)`;
336	  2. `a.mapIso α : a((M.val⊗N.val)⊗P.val) ≅ a(M.val⊗(N.val⊗P.val))`, `α` the
337	     presheaf-of-modules associator;
338	  3. `a(M.val ◁ η_{N.val ⊗ᵖ P.val})` is iso, giving
339	     `a(M.val⊗(N.val⊗P.val)) ≅ M ⊗ (N ⊗ P)`.
340	Steps 1/3 invert the whiskered sheafification unit via the flatness-free
341	`PresheafOfModules.W_whisker{Right,Left}_of_W` (η = `toSheafify ∈ J.W`, and `J.W` is
342	stable under whiskering) together with `isIso_sheafification_map_of_W` (the sheafification
343	functor IS the localization at `J.W.inverseImage (toPresheaf _)`). The defeq carrier bridge
344	`X.ringCatSheaf.val = X.presheaf ⋙ forget₂ CommRingCat RingCat` is handled by the leading
345	`letI instMS` below. -/
346	noncomputable def tensorObj_assoc_iso {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N P : X.Modules} :
347	    tensorObj (tensorObj M N) P ≅ tensorObj M (tensorObj N P) := by
348	  -- UNCONDITIONAL (iter-238, step 0): the locally-trivial hypotheses are dropped —

1692	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1693	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1694	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1695	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1696	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1697	\end{lemma}

1699	\begin{proof}
1700	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1701	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1702	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1703	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1704	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1705	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1706	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1707	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1708	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1709	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1710	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1711	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1712	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1713	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1714	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1715	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1716	
1717	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1718	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1719	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1720	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1721	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1722	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1723	
1724	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1725	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1726	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1727	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1728	  \[
1729	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1730	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1731	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1732	  \]
1733	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1734	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1735	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1736	
1737	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1738	  morphism
1739	  \[
1740	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1741	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1742	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1743	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1744	  \]
1745	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1746	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1747	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1748	  \[
1749	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1750	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1751	  \]
1752	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1753	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1754	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1755	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1756	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1757	
1758	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1759	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1760	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1761	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1762	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1763	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1764	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1765	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1766	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1767	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1768	
1769	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1770	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1771	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1772	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1773	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1774	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1775	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1776	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1777	\end{proof}
1778	
1779	\begin{lemma}

1236	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — D1: the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition
1237	
1238	These are general `PresheafOfModules` statements (any `F : C ⥤ D`, ring presheaves
1239	`R, S`), not specific to schemes; they are project-local because Mathlib's pinned commit
1240	exposes neither `extendScalars` nor `pullback₀` at the presheaf-of-modules level. Both are
1241	realised as the left adjoint of an existing right adjoint: `pushforward₀ F R` is
1242	definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))` (because `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose,
1243	witnessed by Mathlib's `restrictScalars (𝟙 R)).Full := inferInstanceAs (𝟭 _).Full`), and
1244	`restrictScalars φ` is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`; since `pushforward _` is
1245	always a right adjoint, so are these two, and their left adjoints `pullback₀`/`extendScalars`
1246	exist. The decomposition `pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀` then follows from the
1247	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ` by
1248	uniqueness of left adjoints (`Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso`).
1249	
1250	Per blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition` (D1). **OFF-PATH (iter-243 pivot).** D1 is
1251	axiom-clean and self-contained, but it was the first brick of the ABANDONED general
1252	strong-monoidal pullback build (`sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality`, route (e)); the active
1253	route is the loc-triv chart-chase D1'–D4' (§below), which does NOT consume `extendScalars`/
1254	`pullback₀`. Retained as a correct, reusable presheaf-level decomposition; do NOT extend it
1255	toward the general build (D2 scalar-strong / D3 topological-interchange are NOT pursued). -/
1256	
1257	section PullbackLanDecomposition
1258	
1259	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{u} C] {D : Type u} [Category.{u} D]
1260	  {F : C ⥤ D} {R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}} {S : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}}
1261	
1262	/-- `pushforward₀ F R` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))`
1263	(since `restrictScalars (𝟙) = 𝟭` on the nose). Project-local; carries the existence of the
1264	topological inverse image `pullback₀`. -/
1265	private lemma pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1266	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀.{u} F R).IsRightAdjoint :=
1267	  inferInstanceAs (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (𝟙 (F.op ⋙ R))).IsRightAdjoint
1268	
1269	/-- `restrictScalars φ` is a right adjoint: it is definitionally `pushforward (F := 𝟭) φ`.
1270	Project-local; carries the existence of the extension of scalars `extendScalars`. -/
1271	private lemma restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1272	    (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars.{u} φ).IsRightAdjoint :=
1273	  inferInstanceAs
1274	    (PresheafOfModules.pushforward.{u} (F := 𝟭 C) (R := F.op ⋙ R) φ).IsRightAdjoint
1275	
1276	/-- **The topological inverse image `pullback₀ := (pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint`**, the
1277	left adjoint of the fixed-ring pushforward. On underlying presheaves it is the left Kan
1278	extension along `F.op`. Project-local (no `PresheafOfModules` inverse-image functor at the
1279	pin); the carrier of D3. -/
1280	noncomputable def pullback0 (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1281	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} R :=
1282	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1283	  (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R).leftAdjoint
1284	
1285	/-- **Extension of scalars `extendScalars φ := (restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint`**, the left
1286	adjoint of restriction of scalars along a morphism of ring presheaves. Project-local; the
1287	carrier of D2 (strong monoidal via `distribBaseChange`). -/
1288	noncomputable def extendScalars (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1289	    _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} S ⥤ _root_.PresheafOfModules.{u} (F.op ⋙ R) :=
1290	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1291	  (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ).leftAdjoint
1292	
1293	/-- The adjunction `pullback₀ ⊣ pushforward₀`. -/
1294	noncomputable def pullback0Adjunction (F : C ⥤ D) (R : Dᵒᵖ ⥤ RingCat.{u}) :
1295	    pullback0 F R ⊣ PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R :=
1296	  haveI := pushforward₀IsRightAdjoint F R
1297	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.pushforward₀ F R)
1298	
1299	/-- The adjunction `extendScalars φ ⊣ restrictScalars φ`. -/
1300	noncomputable def extendScalarsAdjunction (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1301	    extendScalars φ ⊣ PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ :=
1302	  haveI := restrictScalarsIsRightAdjoint φ
1303	  Adjunction.ofIsRightAdjoint (PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars φ)
1304	
1305	/-- **D1 — the presheaf pullback Lan decomposition** (blueprint `lem:pullback_lan_decomposition`).
1306	For `φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R` presenting a pushforward of presheaves of modules, the presheaf
1307	pullback factors as extension of scalars followed by the topological inverse image,
1308	`pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback₀`. This is the left-adjoint reversal of the
1309	definitional factorisation `pushforward φ = pushforward₀ F R ⋙ restrictScalars φ`, obtained
1310	from `Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso` (uniqueness of left adjoints). Project-local. -/
1311	noncomputable def pullbackLanDecomposition (φ : S ⟶ F.op ⋙ R) :
1312	    PresheafOfModules.pullback φ ≅ extendScalars φ ⋙ pullback0 F R :=
1313	  (Adjunction.leftAdjointCompIso
1314	    (extendScalarsAdjunction φ) (pullback0Adjunction F R)
1315	    (PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction φ)
1316	    (Iso.refl (PresheafOfModules.pushforward φ))).symm
1317	
1318	end PullbackLanDecomposition

3413	[The sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison map $\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}$]
3414	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_map}
3415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorMap}
3416	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3417	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
3418	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
3419	  There is a canonical comparison \emph{morphism}
3420	  \[
3421	    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} :
3422	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,(M \otimes_X N)
3423	      \;\longrightarrow\;
3424	      (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M
3425	        \otimes_Y (\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N
3426	  \]
3427	  of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). It is a \emph{map only}: in
3428	  general it is not asserted to be an isomorphism.
3429	\end{lemma}
3430	
3431	\begin{proof}
3432	  \uses{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3433	  The presheaf pullback is oplax monoidal (\cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}),
3434	  so at the presheaf level it carries the comparison morphism
3435	  \[
3436	    \delta : (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes B)
3437	      \;\longrightarrow\;
3438	      (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
3439	        \otimes (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B,
3440	  \]
3441	  where \(\varphi\) is the ring-presheaf map presenting \(f\). One transports \(\delta\)
3442	  through sheafification to the sheaf level, using the same
3443	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) device that supplies Steps 1--2 of
3444	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}: the abstract pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
3445	  agrees, up to canonical natural isomorphism, with the sheafification of the presheaf
3446	  pullback, and likewise the sheafified tensor product \(\otimes_Y\) is the
3447	  sheafification of the presheaf tensor. The right-hand side reconciliation of the unit
3448	  and tensor factors with their sheafified forms is supplied by the
3449	  sheafification-monoidality brick \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\). Whiskering and
3450	  composing these canonical isomorphisms with the sheafification of \(\delta\) produces
3451	  the asserted sheaf-level morphism \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\), natural in \(M, N\)
3452	  because each ingredient is. No claim of invertibility is made: \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}\)
3453	  is the carrier on which the invertible-case isomorphism argument of
3454	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_pullback} acts.
3455	\end{proof}

3611	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}

3613	  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
3614	  \[

3623	\end{lemma}
3624	
3625	\begin{proof}
3626	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
3627	  This is a one-step consequence of a Mathlib instance, with no chart-chase. The
3628	  canonical comparison map is the genuine Mathlib morphism
3629	  \[
3630	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\,f :
3631	      f^*\mathcal{O}_X \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
3632	  \]
3633	  the unit comparison \(\eta\) of \(f^*\); abbreviate it \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\). Mathlib
3634	  proves this map is an isomorphism whenever the comparison functor
3635	  \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}\) on opens is \(\mathtt{Final}\): the instance
3636	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\).
3637	
3638	  The point is that the finality hypothesis holds \emph{unconditionally}, for every
3639	  morphism of schemes \(f\). The preimage functor \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}
3640	  : \mathtt{Opens}\,X \to \mathtt{Opens}\,Y\) is a frame homomorphism: it preserves
3641	  arbitrary unions and finite intersections, hence preserves all finite limits. A
3642	  functor between small categories that preserves finite limits is representably flat,
3643	  so \(\mathtt{final\_of\_representablyFlat}\) gives \((\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathtt{base}).\mathtt{Final}\)
3644	  with no hypothesis on \(f\) whatsoever. (There is no need to pass to affine charts:
3645	  finality is not a special feature of open immersions but a general property of
3646	  preimage-on-opens.)
3647	
3648	  Consequently the instance \(\mathtt{instIsIsoPullbackObjUnitToUnitOfFinal}\) fires
3649	  directly, and \(\mathrm{pbu}\,f\) is an isomorphism for every \(f\). The asserted
3650	  isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\) is the bundled iso
3651	  \(\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\,f\) of this comparison.

3693	  the oplax-monoidal comparison of the presheaf-level pullback functor

4487	      \(\mathtt{forget}\) (sheaf \(\to\) presheaf) through the composite separates these two
4488	      factors \emph{without} disturbing the left-hand composite unit \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\),
4489	      which is left intact for the final reassembly.
4490	    \item[(c)] \emph{Recover the sub-comparison units.} Apply
4491	      \cref{lem:leftadjointuniq_app_unit_eta_general}
4492	      (\(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniqUnitEta\_app}\), the \(P\)-general step-1 brick) to rewrite
4493	      \(R_1\) as the composite-adjunction unit \(B_f.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,P\) and
4494	      \(R_5\) as \(B_h.\mathrm{unit}.\mathrm{app}\,(\mathtt{PresheafPullback}_f\,P)\). This is
4495	      precisely the uniqueness-of-left-adjoints characterisation
4496	      \(\mathtt{homEquiv\_leftAdjointUniq\_hom\_app}\), now used in its \(P\)-general form.
4497	    \item[(d)] \emph{Slide the \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) comparison past the recovered
4498	      units.} The sheaf-level comparison \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\,h\,f\) is moved across the
4499	      two recovered units \(B_f.\mathrm{unit}\) and \(B_h.\mathrm{unit}\) by its naturality.
4500	    \item[(e)] \emph{Reassemble the composite unit.} Expanding
4501	      \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\) by the composite-adjunction unit formula
4502	      \(\mathtt{comp\_unit\_app}\) and applying \(\mathtt{unit\_naturality}\) identifies the
4503	      slid expression of step~(d) with the left-hand composite unit, closing the tail.
4504	  \end{enumerate}
4505	
4506	  \emph{The precise binding obligation.} The one place where the assembly is not yet a
4507	  mechanical paste is the bridge between the \emph{presheaf}-level left-hand data (the
4508	  presheaf pushforward \(\mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{pre}}\) and the composite unit
4509	  \(B_{h\circ f}.\mathrm{unit}\)) and the \emph{sheaf}-level right-hand factors \(R_1, R_5\)
4510	  wrapped in \(\mathtt{forget}\). Crossing this boundary requires the compatibility
4511	  \[
4512	    \mathtt{forget} \circ \mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{sheaf}}
4513	      \;=\;
4514	    \mathtt{pushforward}^{\mathrm{pre}} \circ \mathtt{forget},
4515	  \]
4516	  interacting with the recovered \(B_f\)- and \(B_h\)-units of step~(c). This compatibility is
4517	  the natural unit to isolate as its own named sub-lemma \emph{before} the assembly: once it
4518	  is in hand, steps (a)--(e) become a mechanical paste, whereas without it the presheaf- and
4519	  sheaf-level pushforwards cannot be aligned at the recovered units. The remainder of the tail
4520	  is bookkeeping around this single compatibility.
4521	
4522	  This is the sheafification-laden analog of \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp}; unlike
4523	  that lemma it is not definitional sectionwise, because the adjunctions involved are
4524	  composite rather than single transposes. The outer Sq1 lemma
4525	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp}\) \emph{consumes} this tail: it peels the leading
4526	  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}\) factor and then invokes
4527	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp\_tail}\) for the residual unit identity.
4528	  \emph{Warning:} transposing the \emph{whole} tail back under the composite adjunction --- as
4529	  opposed to recovering the two sub-units individually via step~(c) and reassembling --- is
4530	  \emph{circular} (verified): it returns the very identity one set out to prove, so the
4531	  step-(c) recovery of the individual \(R_1, R_5\) units is essential.
4532	
4533	  \emph{Sq3 (unit-iso transport).} The monoidal-unit sheafification isomorphism
4534	  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) is carried through the same \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)
4535	  identification used for Sq2.
4536	
4537	  \emph{Sq4 (the connecting iso).} The scheme-level composition coherence of
4538	  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) relates \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,(h \circ f)\,M\) to
4539	  \(h^{*}\bigl(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,M\bigr)\),
4540	  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,h\,(f^{*}M)\), and the pseudofunctoriality component
4541	  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M\); it produces the final
4542	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M,
4543	  (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_N\bigr)\) factor. It is \emph{absent from Mathlib and not yet
4544	  built}; but \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) factors through \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb}\) (as
4545	  \(\mathtt{sheafCompPb}^{-1}\) followed by the pullback of a counit), so its composition
4546	  coherence reduces to that of Sq1 together with the pseudofunctoriality of the counit ---
4547	  i.e.\ Sq4 becomes a short corollary once Sq1 is in hand.
4548	
4549	  This Sq4 coherence is a \emph{standalone named obligation}, to be built separately and not
4550	  folded into Sq1: it is the \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) \emph{composition-coherence} sub-lemma
4551	  --- the naturality across the connecting-object boundary where the abstract pullback's
4552	  \(\mathtt{.val}\) presentation meets the helper-\(\mathtt{.obj}\) presentation --- that is
4553	  consumed inside \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap\_restrict}\). The prover should treat it as its own
4554	  brick (a corollary of Sq1, but with its own statement and its own connecting-object
4555	  bookkeeping), not as a step inlined in the proof of Sq1.
4556	
4557	  The two remaining ingredients are Sq1 (\(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback\_comp}\)) and,
4558	  downstream of it, Sq4: the monoidality Sq2b (\(\mathtt{pullbackComp\_}\delta\) together with
4559	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp\_lax\_}\mu\)) follows from the mate-calculus reduction above, the
4560	  Sq3 unit-iso transport follows from the same \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) identification, and
4561	  the Sq2 ring-map reconciliation is definitional. Sq4 reduces to Sq1
4562	  via the \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) factorisation, so Sq1 is the open target.
4563	  The adjunction-mate bridge \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) (relating
4564	  \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for the left adjoints to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) for the
4565	  right adjoints) is the device used in Sq2b and Sq4 to transport the pseudofunctoriality
4566	  coherence across the adjunctions.
4567	
4568	  The four squares do \emph{not} paste row-by-row. Because \(S_1^{h}\) acts on
4569	  \(\bigl((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr) \otimes
4570	  \bigl((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N\bigr)\), \emph{not} on
4571	  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f}\,(M \otimes N)\), the factors must first be slid past one
4572	  another by \(\delta_{\mathrm{natural}}\) and the naturality of the intervening natural
4573	  transformations before assembly, exactly as in the D1$'$ naturality paste of
4574	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural}. With Sq1 in hand, the Sq2 oplax decomposition, the
4575	  Sq3 unit-iso transport, and the Sq4 connecting-iso coherence assemble the four squares into
4576	  the displayed identity. The base-change-square consequence (the Remark) is then the
4577	  specialisation \(h := j'\) obtained by equating the two factorisations \(j' \mathbin{;} f =
4578	  g \mathbin{;} j\); the iff-clause is immediate, an isomorphism being preserved and reflected
4579	  by the pseudofunctoriality isomorphism it is conjugated by.
4580	\end{proof}
4581
